construct_clusters.name: institutional_and_social_trust
construct_clusters.description: Measures the degree to which individuals trust formal institutions (police) and informal social actors (neighbors, strangers), capturing generalized interpersonal and institutional confidence that underpins civic cooperation.

construct_clusters.name: national_identity_and_pride
construct_clusters.description: Captures the emotional and symbolic attachment respondents feel toward Mexico as a nation, including pride in being Mexican, primary emotions about the country, and the importance of preserving cultural traditions of origin.

construct_clusters.name: subjective_wellbeing_and_life_satisfaction
construct_clusters.description: Measures hedonic and evaluative dimensions of personal wellbeing, including overall happiness, life satisfaction, perceived control over one's future, and confidence in realizing personal projects.

construct_clusters.name: socioeconomic_mobility_and_opportunity_perception
construct_clusters.description: Assesses respondents' perceptions of economic progress across generations and their expectations about future opportunity, reflecting beliefs about social mobility and structural fairness in Mexico.

construct_clusters.name: moral_conservatism_and_social_values
construct_clusters.description: Taps into traditional versus liberal value orientations across family, gender, and ethical domains, including attitudes toward corporal punishment, premarital sex, abortion, same-sex adoption, and self-placement on a conservative-liberal scale.

sources.citation: Rothstein, B., & Stolle, D. (2008). The state and social capital: An institutional theory of generalized trust. Comparative Politics, 40(4), 441–459.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical framework linking institutional quality to generalized social trust, directly relevant to combining institutional (police) and interpersonal (neighbors, strangers) trust items.
sources.key_finding: Impartial and fair government institutions generate generalized social trust by signaling that most people in society can be trusted.

sources.citation: Morris, S. D., & Klesner, J. L. (2010). Corruption and trust: Theoretical considerations and evidence from Mexico. Comparative Political Studies, 43(10), 1258–1285.
sources.relevance: Directly examines the relationship between corruption perceptions and both institutional and interpersonal trust in Mexico using survey data.
sources.key_finding: Corruption perceptions significantly erode both institutional and interpersonal trust in Mexico, and the two forms of trust are reciprocally related.

sources.citation: Zmerli, S., & Newton, K. (2008). Social trust and attitudes toward democracy. Public Opinion Quarterly, 72(4), 706–724.
sources.relevance: Demonstrates the empirical distinction and interplay between social trust and institutional trust as separate but related dimensions of civic confidence.
sources.key_finding: Social trust and political trust are moderately correlated and both independently predict support for democratic governance.

sources.citation: Bargsted, M., Somma, N. M., & Castillo, J. C. (2017). Political trust in Latin America. In S. Zmerli & T. W. G. van der Meer (Eds.), Handbook on Political Trust (pp. 395–417). Edward Elgar.
sources.relevance: Reviews the measurement and determinants of political and social trust across Latin America, providing regional context for the Mexican case.
sources.key_finding: Latin American countries exhibit persistently low levels of institutional trust driven by corruption, inequality, and crime victimization, while interpersonal trust varies more by community context.

sources.citation: Béjar, R., & Rosales, H. (2005). La identidad nacional mexicana como problema político y cultural. Nuevos desafíos. UNAM/CRIM.
sources.relevance: Directly analyzes the political and cultural dimensions of Mexican national identity, including symbolic attachment and pride.
sources.key_finding: Mexican national identity is a multi-layered construct shaped by historical narratives, cultural traditions, and ongoing tensions between globalization and local belonging.

sources.citation: Smith, T. W., & Kim, S. (2006). National pride in comparative perspective: 1995/96 and 2003/04. International Journal of Public Opinion Research, 18(1), 127–136.
sources.relevance: Provides a cross-national framework for measuring national pride, including general and domain-specific pride items similar to those in this construct.
sources.key_finding: National pride varies significantly across countries and is associated with nationalism, patriotism, and sociodemographic factors, with Latin American countries generally scoring high on general national pride.

sources.citation: Gutiérrez Martínez, D. (2010). Sentimiento de pertenencia e identidad nacional en México. In D. Gutiérrez Martínez & H. Balslev Clausen (Eds.), Revisitar la etnicidad (pp. 181–208). Siglo XXI/El Colegio Mexiquense.
sources.relevance: Examines how feelings of belonging and cultural tradition preservation shape national identity in contemporary Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Sense of national belonging in Mexico is strongly tied to cultural practices, regional traditions, and collective memory rather than purely civic or political criteria.

sources.citation: Huddy, L., & Khatib, N. (2007). American patriotism, national identity, and political involvement. American Journal of Political Science, 51(1), 63–77.
sources.relevance: Distinguishes between patriotism and national identity as distinct constructs with different political consequences, informing the measurement of emotional attachment to nation.
sources.key_finding: National identity strength predicts political engagement, while the content of that identity (patriotic vs. nationalistic) determines whether engagement is constructive or exclusionary.

sources.citation: Diener, E., Oishi, S., & Tay, L. (2018). Advances in subjective well-being research. Nature Human Behaviour, 2(4), 253–260.
sources.relevance: Provides the foundational framework for measuring hedonic and evaluative wellbeing, including happiness, life satisfaction, and perceived control—all components of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Subjective wellbeing is a multidimensional construct where life satisfaction, positive affect, and sense of purpose are distinct but correlated components influenced by income, social relationships, and institutional quality.

sources.citation: Rojas, M. (2007). The complexity of well-being in Latin America. In L. Bruni & P. L. Porta (Eds.), Handbook on the Economics of Happiness (pp. 253–271). Edward Elgar.
sources.relevance: Directly examines subjective wellbeing measurement in Latin America, including Mexico, addressing the role of family, social relations, and economic conditions.
sources.key_finding: In Mexico and Latin America, relational and family domains contribute more to subjective wellbeing than income alone, challenging purely economic models of life satisfaction.

sources.citation: Helliwell, J. F., & Putnam, R. D. (2004). The social context of well-being. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B, 359(1449), 1435–1446.
sources.relevance: Establishes the causal link between social trust, community engagement, and subjective wellbeing, connecting this construct to the trust construct in the same survey.
sources.key_finding: Social capital—especially trust and community belonging—is among the strongest predictors of subjective wellbeing, often exceeding the effect of income.

sources.citation: Veenhoven, R. (2012). Happiness: Also known as 'life satisfaction' and 'subjective well-being'. In K. C. Land, A. C. Michalos, & M. J. Sirgy (Eds.), Handbook of Social Indicators and Quality of Life Research (pp. 63–77). Springer.
sources.relevance: Clarifies the conceptual and measurement distinctions between happiness and life satisfaction as used in survey research.
sources.key_finding: Life satisfaction and happiness tap into evaluative and affective dimensions respectively, and both are valid indicators of overall subjective wellbeing when combined.

sources.citation: Torche, F. (2014). Intergenerational mobility and inequality: The Latin American case. Annual Review of Sociology, 40, 619–642.
sources.relevance: Provides comprehensive analysis of social mobility patterns in Latin America including Mexico, directly relevant to the intergenerational progress items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Latin America exhibits lower intergenerational mobility than developed countries, with Mexico showing particularly strong persistence of inequality across generations, especially at the extremes of the distribution.

sources.citation: Alesina, A., Stantcheva, S., & Teso, E. (2018). Intergenerational mobility and preferences for redistribution. American Economic Review, 108(2), 521–554.
sources.relevance: Examines how perceptions of social mobility (as opposed to actual mobility) shape policy preferences, directly relevant to the subjective measurement approach of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Perceived social mobility diverges significantly from actual mobility and is a stronger predictor of attitudes toward redistribution and government intervention than objective mobility data.

sources.citation: Campos-Vázquez, R. M., & Monroy-Gómez-Franco, J. A. (2016). La relación entre crecimiento económico y pobreza en México. Investigación Económica, 75(298), 77–113.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the relationship between economic growth, poverty, and perceived opportunity in Mexico, providing context for how Mexicans assess their economic trajectories.
sources.key_finding: Economic growth in Mexico has not translated proportionally into poverty reduction or perceived upward mobility, suggesting structural barriers that shape pessimistic opportunity perceptions among lower-income groups.

sources.citation: Benabou, R., & Ok, E. A. (2001). Social mobility and the demand for redistribution: The POUM hypothesis. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 116(2), 447–487.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical foundation (Prospect of Upward Mobility hypothesis) for understanding how future mobility expectations shape current political and economic attitudes.
sources.key_finding: Individuals who expect upward mobility for themselves or their children are less likely to support redistribution, even when currently disadvantaged.

sources.citation: Inglehart, R., & Welzel, C. (2005). Modernization, cultural change, and democracy: The human development sequence. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides the foundational theory of traditional vs. secular-rational values and survival vs. self-expression values, directly relevant to measuring conservative-liberal value orientations in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Economic development drives a shift from traditional/survival values toward secular-rational/self-expression values, but cultural heritage (e.g., Catholic Latin American) creates persistent path dependencies in value change.

sources.citation: Díez, J. (2015). The politics of gay marriage in Latin America: Argentina, Chile, and Mexico. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Directly examines attitudes toward same-sex relationships and adoption in Mexico, including the cultural, religious, and political factors shaping moral conservatism.
sources.key_finding: Support for same-sex rights in Mexico is stratified by religiosity, education, urbanization, and generational cohort, with younger, urban, and more educated Mexicans showing significantly more liberal attitudes.

sources.citation: Moreno, A. (2005). Nuestros valores: Los mexicanos en México y en Estados Unidos al inicio del siglo XXI. Banamex.
sources.relevance: Directly measures and analyzes Mexican value orientations including family values, gender attitudes, and moral conservatism using nationally representative survey data.
sources.key_finding: Mexican society exhibits a complex coexistence of traditional family and moral values with increasing acceptance of individual autonomy, particularly among younger and more urban populations.

sources.citation: Pew Research Center. (2014). Religion in Latin America: Widespread change in a historically Catholic region. Pew Research Center.
sources.relevance: Documents the relationship between religious affiliation/practice and moral attitudes across Latin America, including Mexico, on issues like abortion, homosexuality, and family structure.
sources.key_finding: In Mexico, evangelical Protestants hold more conservative moral views than Catholics on issues like abortion and homosexuality, while the religiously unaffiliated are substantially more liberal.

sources.citation: Schwartz, S. H. (2012). An overview of the Schwartz theory of basic values. Online Readings in Psychology and Culture, 2(1), 11.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical basis for understanding conservation vs. openness-to-change value dimensions that underlie moral conservatism scales.
sources.key_finding: Conservation values (tradition, conformity, security) systematically oppose openness-to-change values (self-direction, stimulation), and this dimension is a universal axis of individual and cultural variation.

variable_strategies.justification: Institutional and social trust is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning trust in specific institutions and generalized social trust, so no single item captures the full construct. Items p50_1, p48, p49, and p40 all share a compatible 6-category scale, making them directly combinable into a mean score that can be binned into ≤5 ordinal categories. Items p51_1 and p52_1 are excluded because their 13-category scales are incompatible with the 6-point items and would distort the composite without standardization; the 6-point items alone provide sufficient breadth across institutional and interpersonal trust dimensions.

National identity and pride is a multi-faceted construct best captured by combining items that tap affective pride and identity salience rather than relying on a single indicator. Items p3_2 (6 categories) and p78 (5 categories) are the closest in scale and most face-valid for subjective national pride and identification, while p5_1a, p6, p8, and p9 appear to tap more specific behavioral or categorical identity dimensions with lower entropy and potentially incompatible scales. Averaging p3_2 and p78 and binning to 5 ordinal categories yields a parsimonious, content-valid composite that reduces single-item measurement error for this high-importance construct.

Subjective wellbeing is a canonical multi-component construct encompassing hedonic and evaluative dimensions, so aggregation is strongly preferred over any single item. Items p20, p24, p30_1, and p25_2 all use 6- or 7-category scales that are close enough in range to be averaged after rescaling to a common 0–1 interval, then binned to 5 ordinal quantile categories. Items p29 and p74 are excluded because their 13-category scales would dominate the composite and introduce scale incompatibility; the four selected items already span life satisfaction, affect, and domain-specific wellbeing adequately for a bridge variable.

Perceived socioeconomic mobility and opportunity is a latent construct requiring multiple items to capture both retrospective mobility assessments and prospective opportunity beliefs. Items p18, p19, p22, and p23_1 share a 5-category scale, and p11 uses a 7-category scale that is close enough to be rescaled to 0–1 before averaging; together they cover upward mobility expectations, perceived fairness of opportunity, and current economic situation. Item p27 (11 categories) is excluded to avoid scale incompatibility, and the five selected items provide sufficient construct coverage after mean-scoring and binning to 5 ordinal categories.

Moral conservatism is a broad latent dimension best measured by combining multiple attitudinal items rather than relying on a single question, given the construct's importance rating and multi-item availability. Items p55, p58, p60, and p71_2 all use a uniform 5-category scale, making them directly summable and mean-scorable without rescaling, and they collectively tap traditional moral attitudes across social and ethical domains. Items with 7- or 8-category scales (p56, p57, p66_1, p68, p67) are excluded to maintain scale homogeneity; the four 5-point items provide a clean, internally consistent composite that can be binned into 5 ordinal categories for the bridge model.

construct_clusters.name: environmental_knowledge_literacy
construct_clusters.description: Measures the respondent's self-assessed and objective knowledge about environmental problems, their causes, solutions, and renewable energy, capturing environmental literacy as a latent cognitive construct.

construct_clusters.name: environmental_concern_risk_perception
construct_clusters.description: Captures the degree to which respondents perceive environmental problems as serious threats at national and local levels, including concern about specific issues such as climate change and fossil fuel impacts.

construct_clusters.name: pro_environmental_civic_engagement
construct_clusters.description: Measures the extent to which respondents have taken collective or civic action to address environmental issues, reflecting behavioral mobilization beyond individual lifestyle choices.

construct_clusters.name: environmental_governance_attitudes
construct_clusters.description: Captures respondents' beliefs about who bears responsibility for environmental protection and the appropriate role of government and companies in regulating environmental behavior.

construct_clusters.name: socioeconomic_environmental_context
construct_clusters.description: Measures the material and infrastructural conditions of respondents' households and communities that shape both environmental exposure and adaptive capacity, serving as a proxy for socio-environmental vulnerability.

sources.citation: Dunlap, R. E., & Jones, R. E. (2002). Environmental concern: Conceptual and measurement issues. In R. E. Dunlap & W. Michelson (Eds.), Handbook of environmental sociology (pp. 482–524). Greenwood Press.
sources.relevance: Provides foundational framework for distinguishing environmental knowledge from concern and behavior, establishing environmental literacy as a distinct cognitive dimension in survey research.
sources.key_finding: Environmental knowledge is a necessary but insufficient predictor of pro-environmental behavior, operating through mediating attitudinal pathways.

sources.citation: Tobler, C., Visschers, V. H. M., & Siegrist, M. (2012). Consumers' knowledge about climate change. Climatic Change, 114(2), 189–209.
sources.relevance: Directly measures self-assessed and objective knowledge about climate change causes and solutions, closely mirroring the construct's inclusion of knowledge about environmental problems and renewable energy.
sources.key_finding: Objective knowledge about climate change causes was a stronger predictor of willingness to act than self-assessed knowledge, highlighting the importance of measuring both dimensions.

sources.citation: Fernández-Manzanal, R., Rodríguez-Barreiro, L., & Carrasquer, J. (2007). Evaluation of environmental attitudes: Analysis and results of a scale applied to university students. Science Education, 91(6), 988–1009.
sources.relevance: Validates multi-item scales for environmental literacy including knowledge of causes and solutions to environmental problems in a Spanish-speaking educational context.
sources.key_finding: Environmental knowledge scales showed acceptable reliability and were positively associated with pro-environmental attitudes among Spanish-speaking university students.

sources.citation: Liere, K. D. V., & Dunlap, R. E. (1980). The social bases of environmental concern: A review of hypotheses, explanations and empirical evidence. Public Opinion Quarterly, 44(2), 181–197.
sources.relevance: Reviews the sociodemographic predictors of environmental knowledge and concern, establishing education and age as key correlates of environmental literacy.
sources.key_finding: Education is the most consistent predictor of environmental knowledge and concern across multiple studies.

sources.citation: Dunlap, R. E., Van Liere, K. D., Mertig, A. G., & Jones, R. E. (2000). New trends in measuring environmental attitudes: Measuring endorsement of the New Ecological Paradigm: A revised NEP scale. Journal of Social Issues, 56(3), 425–442.
sources.relevance: The revised NEP scale is the most widely used instrument for measuring environmental concern, and its dimensions of ecological limits and human-nature balance directly inform the concern/risk perception construct.
sources.key_finding: The revised NEP scale demonstrates strong internal consistency and predictive validity for environmental attitudes and behaviors across diverse populations.

sources.citation: Leiserowitz, A. (2006). Climate change risk perception and policy preferences: The role of affect, imagery, and values. Climatic Change, 77(1–2), 45–72.
sources.relevance: Directly examines how risk perception of climate change shapes policy preferences, closely matching the construct's focus on perceived seriousness of climate change and fossil fuel impacts.
sources.key_finding: Risk perception of climate change is strongly influenced by affective imagery and cultural values, and is a primary driver of support for mitigation policies.

sources.citation: Ponce de León Atria, M., & Escotto Quesada, T. (2014). Percepción social del cambio climático en México. In B. Ortiz & C. Velasco (Eds.), La percepción social del cambio climático (pp. 73–94). Universidad Iberoamericana.
sources.relevance: One of the few studies examining environmental risk perception specifically in the Mexican context, analyzing how Mexicans perceive climate change threats at national and local levels.
sources.key_finding: Mexican respondents show high levels of concern about climate change but significant variation by region and socioeconomic status, with local environmental degradation amplifying risk perception.

sources.citation: Brody, S. D., Zahran, S., Vedlitz, A., & Grover, H. (2008). Examining the relationship between physical vulnerability and public perceptions of global climate change in the United States. Environment and Behavior, 40(1), 72–95.
sources.relevance: Examines how local environmental conditions and physical vulnerability shape perceptions of climate change risk, relevant to the construct's inclusion of both national and local threat perception.
sources.key_finding: Individuals living in areas with greater physical vulnerability to climate impacts report significantly higher levels of climate change risk perception.

sources.citation: Weber, E. U. (2006). Experience-based and description-based perceptions of long-term risk: Why global warming does not scare us (yet). Climatic Change, 77(1–2), 103–120.
sources.relevance: Provides theoretical grounding for why environmental risk perception varies, distinguishing between experiential and analytical processing of environmental threats.
sources.key_finding: Personal experience with environmental hazards is a more powerful driver of risk perception than statistical information, explaining variation in concern across populations.

causal_relationships.related_construct: support for environmental policy and regulation

sources.citation: Stern, P. C. (2000). New environmental theories: Toward a coherent theory of environmentally significant behavior. Journal of Social Issues, 56(3), 407–424.
sources.relevance: Stern's Value-Belief-Norm theory distinguishes between private-sphere and public-sphere (civic) environmental behaviors, directly informing the construct's focus on collective action beyond individual lifestyle choices.
sources.key_finding: Public-sphere environmental behaviors (activism, policy support, civic engagement) are driven by a distinct causal chain from personal norms activated by awareness of consequences and ascription of responsibility.

sources.citation: Lubell, M. (2002). Environmental activism as collective action. Environment and Behavior, 34(4), 431–454.
sources.relevance: Applies collective action theory to environmental civic engagement, examining why individuals participate in environmental protests, organizations, and political actions.
sources.key_finding: Perceived collective benefits, civic duty norms, and social trust significantly predict participation in environmental collective action, overcoming free-rider problems.

sources.citation: Hadler, M., & Haller, M. (2011). Global activism and nationally driven recycling: The influence of world society and national contexts on public and private environmental behavior. International Sociology, 26(3), 315–345.
sources.relevance: Compares civic environmental engagement across countries, showing how national context shapes the relationship between attitudes and collective environmental action.
sources.key_finding: National-level factors such as democratic governance and civil society strength moderate the translation of environmental concern into civic engagement.

sources.citation: Leff, E. (2012). Political ecology in Latin America: The social re-appropriation of nature, the reinvention of territories and the construction of an environmental rationality. Desenvolvimento e Meio Ambiente, 35, 65–88.
sources.relevance: Provides a Latin American theoretical framework for understanding environmental civic mobilization in the context of resource conflicts, indigenous rights, and social movements in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Environmental civic engagement in Latin America is deeply intertwined with social justice movements, territorial defense, and indigenous epistemologies, distinguishing it from Northern environmentalism.

causal_relationships.related_construct: indigenous community membership and collective action

sources.citation: Dryzek, J. S. (2013). The politics of the earth: Environmental discourses (3rd ed.). Oxford University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive typology of environmental governance discourses—including regulatory, market-based, and participatory approaches—that frame how publics assign responsibility for environmental protection.
sources.key_finding: Public attitudes toward environmental governance reflect deeper ideological commitments about the appropriate role of the state, markets, and civil society in managing environmental commons.

sources.citation: Fairbrother, M. (2016). Trust and public support for environmental protection in diverse national contexts. Sociological Science, 3, 359–382.
sources.relevance: Examines how trust in government moderates public support for environmental regulation, directly relevant to the construct's focus on beliefs about governmental responsibility.
sources.key_finding: Higher trust in government is associated with greater support for environmental regulation, but this relationship is stronger in countries with lower corruption.

sources.citation: Lezama, J. L. (2010). Sociedad, medio ambiente y política ambiental, 1970–2000. In J. L. Lezama & B. Graizbord (Eds.), Los grandes problemas de México, Vol. IV: Medio ambiente (pp. 23–60). El Colegio de México.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the evolution of environmental governance attitudes in Mexico, including public expectations of government responsibility and the role of SEMARNAT and other institutions.
sources.key_finding: Mexican environmental governance attitudes are shaped by a legacy of centralized state control over natural resources, creating high expectations of government responsibility alongside deep skepticism about enforcement capacity.

sources.citation: Harring, N. (2013). Understanding the effects of corruption and political trust on willingness to make economic sacrifices for environmental protection in a cross-national perspective. Social Science Quarterly, 94(3), 660–671.
sources.relevance: Directly tests how corruption perceptions affect willingness to support environmental governance measures, relevant to the Mexican context where corruption is a salient concern.
sources.key_finding: Perceived corruption significantly reduces willingness to accept economic costs for environmental protection, as citizens doubt that government will use resources effectively.

causal_relationships.related_construct: attitudes toward economic regulation and market role

sources.citation: Cutter, S. L., Boruff, B. J., & Shirley, W. L. (2003). Social vulnerability to environmental hazards. Social Science Quarterly, 84(2), 242–261.
sources.relevance: Develops the Social Vulnerability Index (SoVI) framework, which conceptualizes socioeconomic and infrastructural conditions as determinants of environmental vulnerability, directly paralleling this construct's proxy for socio-environmental vulnerability.
sources.key_finding: Social vulnerability to environmental hazards is a composite of socioeconomic status, housing quality, infrastructure access, and demographic characteristics that determine differential exposure and adaptive capacity.

sources.citation: Sánchez-Rodríguez, R. (2002). Sustentabilidad urbana, descentralización y gestión local. In E. Leff, E. Ezcurra, I. Pisanty, & P. Romero Lankao (Eds.), La transición hacia el desarrollo sustentable (pp. 293–314). SEMARNAT/INE/UAM/PNUMA.
sources.relevance: Examines how urban infrastructure, housing conditions, and municipal governance in Mexico create differential environmental vulnerability, directly relevant to the construct's measurement of household and community conditions.
sources.key_finding: Uneven urban development in Mexico creates pockets of extreme socio-environmental vulnerability where lack of basic services (water, sanitation, waste management) compounds exposure to environmental hazards.

sources.citation: Adger, W. N. (2006). Vulnerability. Global Environmental Change, 16(3), 268–281.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical framework linking material conditions (exposure, sensitivity, adaptive capacity) to environmental vulnerability, justifying the use of household infrastructure as a proxy measure.
sources.key_finding: Vulnerability is a function of exposure to environmental stressors, sensitivity of the system, and adaptive capacity, all of which are shaped by socioeconomic conditions and infrastructure.

sources.citation: CONEVAL. (2018). Estudio diagnóstico del derecho a un medio ambiente sano. Consejo Nacional de Evaluación de la Política de Desarrollo Social.
sources.relevance: Provides Mexico-specific data on how poverty, housing quality, and access to basic services (water, sanitation) intersect with environmental quality and exposure to hazards.
sources.key_finding: In Mexico, the poorest municipalities face disproportionate environmental degradation and lack of basic environmental services, creating a reinforcing cycle of poverty and environmental vulnerability.

causal_relationships.related_construct: health outcomes and health risk exposure
housing quality and access to services

variable_strategies.justification: Environmental knowledge/literacy is inherently multi-dimensional, requiring aggregation to capture breadth of understanding. The three highest-entropy items (p8 H=3.16, p9 H=3.24, p29 H=3.35) are selected as they carry the most discriminating information and likely tap distinct knowledge facets; p27_1, p28_1, and p36_1 have substantially lower entropy and fewer categories, risking floor effects. PCA first component is preferred over sum/mean because the items have different scale widths (all K=13 but potentially different response distributions), and PCA extracts the shared latent variance without assuming equal item weights.

Environmental concern and risk perception is a well-established multi-item construct where no single question captures both affective concern and cognitive risk appraisal; aggregation reduces measurement error and increases reliability. Items p4 and p5 (K=7) likely tap concern intensity, p6 and p7 (K=11) tap risk magnitude or breadth, and p38_1 (K=7, H=2.16) adds a complementary concern dimension; p30_1 (H=1.51) and p37 (H=1.49) are excluded due to low entropy indicating poor discrimination. Mean scoring is appropriate because items share broadly ordinal Likert-type structures and the mean is robust to the slight K differences (7 vs 11), with subsequent binning to ≤5 categories for the bridge model.

All five items share an identical battery structure (K=4, very similar H≈0.50–0.70), strongly indicating they are a designed Likert battery measuring different civic engagement behaviors that should be summed into a total engagement score. No single item dominates in entropy or content validity since they are parallel indicators of the same behavioral domain. Sum scoring is the standard approach for behavioral checklists of this type, and the narrow entropy range confirms comparable item difficulty, making a simple sum appropriate before binning.

Environmental governance attitudes span trust in institutions, policy support, and regulatory preferences, requiring multiple items to cover the construct adequately. Items p12_1 (K=7, H=1.77), p13 (K=5, H=1.62), and p40 (K=6, H=1.84) provide the best entropy and likely cover distinct governance sub-dimensions; p41 is excluded due to very low entropy (H=0.97), suggesting a near-uniform or highly skewed response that would add noise rather than signal. Mean scoring accommodates the slight scale-width differences (K=5,6,7) by normalizing implicitly through averaging, and the result is binned to ≤5 ordinal categories for the bridge.

Socioeconomic-environmental context is a latent composite of material conditions, neighborhood environment quality, and resource access that no single item captures; aggregation is essential. Items p46_1 (H=2.40), p42 (H=2.23), p47 (H=1.79), and p39 (H=1.59) are selected as the four highest-entropy indicators, while p25_1 (H=0.83), p26_1 (H=0.92), and p44 (H=1.12) are excluded for low discrimination. PCA first component is preferred because the items span different K values (6 and 8) and likely have heterogeneous inter-correlations reflecting distinct socioeconomic dimensions, so PCA extracts the dominant shared variance without imposing equal weights.

construct_clusters.name: subjective_social_mobility
construct_clusters.description: Captures respondents' perceived trajectory of economic and social standing across generations — backward-looking (own situation vs. parents), forward-looking (children vs. self), and structural beliefs about whether upward mobility is realistically achievable for young Mexicans today.

construct_clusters.name: material_living_standards
construct_clusters.description: Measures objective household material conditions through dwelling quality and asset ownership, forming a composite index of socioeconomic deprivation or well-being consistent with multidimensional poverty measurement frameworks.

construct_clusters.name: structural_attribution_of_poverty
construct_clusters.description: Assesses the degree to which respondents attribute poverty and social problems to systemic, institutional, or structural forces (e.g., government, elites, discrimination) rather than individual failings, reflecting ideological orientations toward inequality.

construct_clusters.name: social_policy_preferences
construct_clusters.description: Captures respondents' normative preferences regarding how anti-poverty policy should be designed, funded, prioritized, and delivered, including beliefs about government effectiveness and the appropriate role of different societal actors.

construct_clusters.name: subjective_economic_wellbeing
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' self-assessed economic and psychological well-being, including perceived personal control over economic outcomes, happiness, satisfaction with work and leisure, self-identified social class, and perceived resilience — capturing the subjective experience of living standards beyond objective material conditions.

sources.citation: Campos-Vázquez, R. M., & Vélez-Grajales, R. (2014). Crisis económica y percepciones de movilidad social en México. Sobre México: Temas de Economía, 1(1), 54-78.
sources.relevance: Directly examines perceived social mobility in Mexico, including intergenerational comparisons and beliefs about upward mobility prospects.
sources.key_finding: Economic crises significantly reduce perceived upward mobility, with lower-income Mexicans reporting the sharpest declines in optimism about intergenerational progress.

sources.citation: Torche, F. (2014). Intergenerational mobility and inequality: The Latin American case. Annual Review of Sociology, 40, 619-642.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive framework for understanding both objective and subjective intergenerational mobility in Latin America, including Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Latin America exhibits moderate intergenerational mobility at the middle of the distribution but strong persistence at the extremes, and subjective perceptions often diverge from objective patterns.

sources.citation: Kelley, S. M. C., & Kelley, C. G. E. (2009). Subjective social mobility: Data from 30 nations. In M. Haller, R. Jowell, & T. W. Smith (Eds.), The International Social Survey Programme 1984-2009 (pp. 106-124). Routledge.
sources.relevance: Establishes measurement approaches for subjective social mobility using retrospective and prospective intergenerational comparisons, directly paralleling the construct's question structure.
sources.key_finding: Subjective social mobility is shaped by both actual economic conditions and national cultural narratives about opportunity, with developing countries showing higher perceived upward mobility despite greater inequality.

sources.citation: Vélez-Grajales, R., Campos-Vázquez, R. M., & Huerta-Wong, J. E. (2014). Report on social mobility in Mexico: Imagine your future. Centro de Estudios Espinosa Yglesias (CEEY).
sources.relevance: The foundational Mexican study on social mobility perceptions, combining objective and subjective measures and examining beliefs about whether effort leads to upward mobility.
sources.key_finding: A majority of Mexicans perceive themselves as having experienced upward mobility relative to their parents, but a significant share believe structural barriers prevent young people from achieving similar progress.

sources.citation: Alesina, A., Stantcheva, S., & Teso, E. (2018). Intergenerational mobility and preferences for redistribution. American Economic Review, 108(2), 521-554.
sources.relevance: Demonstrates the causal link between perceived social mobility and policy preferences, providing theoretical grounding for connecting this construct to social policy attitudes.
sources.key_finding: Pessimistic perceptions of social mobility increase support for redistributive policies, and these perceptions are shaped by personal experience, media, and national narratives.

sources.citation: CONEVAL. (2019). Metodología para la medición multidimensional de la pobreza en México (3ra edición). Consejo Nacional de Evaluación de la Política de Desarrollo Social.
sources.relevance: The official Mexican methodology for multidimensional poverty measurement, which includes dwelling quality and asset ownership as core dimensions, directly mirroring this construct.
sources.key_finding: Material deprivation indicators (housing quality, basic services, assets) are essential complements to income-based poverty measures and capture distinct dimensions of well-being in Mexico.

sources.citation: Alkire, S., & Foster, J. (2011). Counting and multidimensional poverty measurement. Journal of Public Economics, 95(7-8), 476-487.
sources.relevance: Provides the foundational methodology (AF method) for constructing composite indices of material deprivation from dwelling and asset indicators, widely adopted in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Multidimensional poverty indices that combine housing quality, asset ownership, and service access provide more policy-relevant poverty identification than income alone.

sources.citation: Filmer, D., & Pritchett, L. H. (2001). Estimating wealth effects without expenditure data—or tears: An application to educational enrollments in states of India. Demography, 38(1), 115-132.
sources.relevance: Establishes the asset-index approach to measuring household socioeconomic status from survey data on dwelling characteristics and durable goods, a method widely used in developing-country surveys.
sources.key_finding: A principal-components-based asset index constructed from housing quality and ownership variables is a robust proxy for long-run household wealth.

sources.citation: McKenzie, D. J. (2005). Measuring inequality with asset indicators. Journal of Population Economics, 18(2), 229-260.
sources.relevance: Evaluates the validity and limitations of asset-based measures of living standards for inequality analysis in Mexico and other developing countries.
sources.key_finding: Asset indices capture durable aspects of living standards well but may underestimate inequality at the top and bottom of the distribution compared to consumption measures.

causal_relationships.related_construct: digital access and information society participation

sources.citation: Feagin, J. R. (1975). Subordinating the poor: Welfare and American beliefs. Prentice-Hall.
sources.relevance: Foundational work establishing the tripartite framework of poverty attributions: individualistic, structural, and fatalistic, which underpins this construct's measurement approach.
sources.key_finding: Americans tend to favor individualistic explanations for poverty, but structural attributions are significantly more common among lower-income and minority respondents.

sources.citation: Rojas-Méndez, R. (2008). Percepciones sobre las causas de la pobreza en México. In R. Cordera & C. J. Cabrera (Eds.), La política social en México: Tendencias y perspectivas. UNAM.
sources.relevance: Directly examines Mexican public attributions for poverty, distinguishing structural from individual explanations in the national context.
sources.key_finding: Mexican respondents who attribute poverty to structural causes (government corruption, lack of opportunities, discrimination) are significantly more likely to support expansive social programs.

sources.citation: Oorschot, W. V., & Halman, L. (2000). The individual and structural causes of poverty: A European comparison. In W. Arts & L. Halman (Eds.), New directions in quantitative comparative sociology (pp. 169-190). Brill.
sources.relevance: Provides cross-national evidence on how structural vs. individual poverty attributions vary by welfare regime and cultural context, offering comparative benchmarks.
sources.key_finding: Structural poverty attributions are more prevalent in countries with higher inequality and weaker welfare states, consistent with the Latin American context.

sources.citation: Merolla, D. M., Hunt, M. O., & Serpe, R. T. (2011). Concentrated disadvantage and beliefs about the causes of poverty: A multi-level analysis. Sociological Perspectives, 54(2), 205-227.
sources.relevance: Demonstrates how neighborhood-level deprivation shapes individual-level structural attributions, relevant to understanding Mexican spatial inequality.
sources.key_finding: Living in areas of concentrated poverty increases structural attributions, net of individual socioeconomic status, suggesting contextual effects on causal beliefs.

causal_relationships.related_construct: social policy preferences and support for redistribution
personal experience of poverty and deprivation
attitudes toward discrimination and human rights

sources.citation: Díaz-Cayeros, A., Estévez, F., & Magaloni, B. (2016). The political logic of poverty relief: Electoral strategies and social policy in Mexico. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes Mexican public preferences for anti-poverty programs in the context of electoral politics, examining how citizens evaluate government effectiveness and program design.
sources.key_finding: Mexican voters' preferences for social policy design are shaped by both programmatic evaluations of effectiveness and clientelistic political relationships, with conditional cash transfers gaining broad support.

sources.citation: Blofield, M., & Martínez Franzoni, J. (2015). Maternalism, co-responsibility, and social equity: A typology of work–family policies. Social Politics, 22(1), 38-59.
sources.relevance: Provides a framework for understanding normative preferences about the role of state vs. family vs. market in social provision, applicable to Mexican policy attitudes.
sources.key_finding: Public preferences for social policy design reflect underlying beliefs about gender roles, state responsibility, and market adequacy, varying systematically across Latin American countries.

sources.citation: Ardanaz, M., & Scartascini, C. (2013). Inequality and personal income tax compliance: Evidence from Latin America. IDB Working Paper No. 390.
sources.relevance: Examines willingness to fund social programs through taxation in Latin America, directly relevant to questions about how anti-poverty policy should be financed.
sources.key_finding: Higher perceived inequality increases stated willingness to pay taxes for social programs, but only when citizens trust that government will use revenues effectively.

sources.citation: Garay, C. (2016). Social policy expansion in Latin America. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the political determinants of social policy expansion in Latin America, providing context for understanding citizen preferences about policy scope and targeting.
sources.key_finding: Public demand for social policy expansion in Latin America is driven by both material need and normative beliefs about state responsibility, with cross-class coalitions supporting universal over targeted approaches.

sources.citation: Zechmeister, E. J., & Corral, M. (2013). Individual and contextual constraints on ideological labels in Latin America. Comparative Political Studies, 46(6), 675-701.
sources.relevance: Demonstrates how ideological self-placement relates to policy preferences in Latin America, relevant to understanding the ideological structure of social policy attitudes in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Ideological labels in Latin America map onto social policy preferences, but the mapping is weaker and more context-dependent than in advanced democracies.

causal_relationships.related_construct: gender role attitudes and women's economic participation

sources.citation: Rojas, M. (2008). Experienced poverty and income poverty in Mexico: A subjective well-being approach. World Development, 36(6), 1078-1093.
sources.relevance: Directly examines subjective economic well-being in Mexico, demonstrating that self-assessed economic satisfaction captures dimensions of poverty missed by income measures.
sources.key_finding: Subjective economic well-being in Mexico is only moderately correlated with income, and factors such as social comparison, perceived control, and family relationships independently predict life satisfaction among the poor.

sources.citation: Graham, C., & Pettinato, S. (2002). Frustrated achievers: Winners, losers, and subjective well-being in new market economies. Journal of Development Studies, 38(4), 100-140.
sources.relevance: Introduces the concept of 'frustrated achievers' — people whose objective conditions improve but subjective well-being declines — highly relevant to understanding the disconnect between material and subjective measures.
sources.key_finding: In Latin America, rapid economic change creates a gap between objective mobility and subjective satisfaction, with relative deprivation and rising aspirations undermining well-being gains.

sources.citation: Lora, E. (Ed.). (2008). Beyond facts: Understanding quality of life. Inter-American Development Bank.
sources.relevance: Comprehensive IDB study of subjective well-being in Latin America covering satisfaction with work, health, leisure, and economic situation — dimensions directly measured in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Latin Americans report higher subjective well-being than predicted by their income levels, but domain-specific satisfaction (work, economic situation) is more closely tied to objective conditions than overall life satisfaction.

sources.citation: Diener, E., Oishi, S., & Lucas, R. E. (2003). Personality, culture, and subjective well-being: Emotional and cognitive evaluations of life. Annual Review of Psychology, 54, 403-425.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical framework for measuring subjective well-being through multiple domains (happiness, life satisfaction, domain satisfaction, perceived control), which this construct operationalizes.
sources.key_finding: Subjective well-being is a multidimensional construct requiring measurement of both cognitive evaluations (life satisfaction, domain satisfaction) and affective components (happiness), as these tap distinct psychological processes.

sources.citation: Palomar Lever, J. (2005). Percepción de las causas de la pobreza, factores psicológicos asociados y percepción de la movilidad social. In M. Székely (Ed.), Desmitificando la pobreza en México. PNUD/SEDESOL.
sources.relevance: Examines psychological correlates of poverty in Mexico, including locus of control, self-efficacy, and subjective class identification — dimensions captured in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Internal locus of control and self-efficacy are significantly associated with both subjective well-being and actual economic outcomes among poor Mexicans, suggesting psychological resources mediate the poverty-well-being link.

causal_relationships.related_construct: material living standards and objective poverty
employment quality and labor market conditions

variable_strategies.justification: Subjective social mobility is inherently multi-dimensional, encompassing retrospective, current, and prospective assessments of one's social position, and no single item fully captures this breadth. All five questions use similar ordinal ladder/rank scales (6–8 categories) with moderate-to-good entropy, making them combinable; PCA extracts the dominant shared variance across past, present, and future mobility perceptions while discarding item-specific noise. Binning the first principal component into 5 quantiles yields a compact ordinal variable that maximizes construct coverage for the bridge regression.

Material living standards is a multi-dimensional construct spanning income, asset ownership, housing quality, and consumption capacity, so aggregation is strongly preferred over any single item. p47 (15 categories, H=2.59), p53 (H=1.87), p54 (H=1.55), p52 (H=1.45), and p51 (H=1.13) are selected as the highest-entropy and most content-valid items covering distinct material dimensions; p48, p49, and p55 are excluded due to very low entropy (≤1.10 bits) indicating near-constant responses that would distort a sum or mean. PCA on these five items captures the dominant axis of material deprivation/affluence and is robust to their differing scale widths, with the first component binned to 5 quantiles to satisfy the ≤15 category constraint.

Structural attribution of poverty requires capturing the degree to which respondents blame systemic/societal factors versus individual factors, a latent attitude that is best measured by multiple items to reduce acquiescence and item-specific bias. p27_1 (H=2.82), p39 (H=2.29), p40 (H=1.67), p33 (H=1.47), and p32 (H=1.13) are selected for their higher entropy and face validity as attitudinal items about causes of poverty; p37 is excluded because its very low entropy (H=0.55) indicates a near-degenerate distribution that would suppress variance in any composite. PCA is preferred over a sum score because the items span different scale widths (4–10 categories), and the first component will reflect the shared structural-vs-individual attribution dimension after standardization.

Social policy preferences span support for redistribution, targeted transfers, public services, and state intervention, making it a genuinely multi-dimensional construct that no single item can represent adequately. p29_1 (H=2.35), p42 (H=2.37), p31 (H=2.64), p30 (H=2.28), p43 (H=2.18), and p41 (H=1.84) are selected as the six highest-entropy items covering distinct policy domains; p44 (H=1.20) is excluded as its lower entropy and narrower scale (4 categories) add little discriminating information. PCA handles the mixed scale widths (6–11 categories) by standardizing items before extraction, and binning the first component into 5 quantiles produces a valid ordinal bridge variable reflecting the dominant pro- vs. anti-redistribution attitude dimension.

Subjective economic wellbeing is a latent construct encompassing perceived income adequacy, financial stress, ability to make ends meet, and economic expectations, all of which are distinct facets requiring multiple indicators for reliable measurement. All seven selected items use near-identical 12–13 category scales with high entropy (H ranging from 2.36 to 3.16 bits), making them highly compatible for aggregation; p10 (K=7, H=1.98) is excluded because its narrower scale is incompatible with the dominant 13-point scale family and would introduce scale-mixing artifacts. PCA on the seven homogeneous items extracts the primary subjective wellbeing dimension with maximum fidelity, and binning to 5 quantiles produces a parsimonious ordinal variable well-suited for the bridge regression.

construct_clusters.name: democratic_legitimacy
construct_clusters.description: Measures the degree to which respondents value, trust, and are satisfied with democracy as a system of government in Mexico, capturing both normative commitment to democracy and evaluations of its actual functioning.

construct_clusters.name: institutional_trust_and_efficacy
construct_clusters.description: Captures respondents' trust in key political and social institutions and their belief in citizens' capacity to influence government decisions, reflecting both external political efficacy and institutional confidence.

construct_clusters.name: civic_and_political_participation
construct_clusters.description: Measures the extent and forms of respondents' engagement in political and civic life, including voting behavior, community organizing, and attitudes toward participation as a meaningful activity.

construct_clusters.name: political_interest_and_engagement
construct_clusters.description: Assesses respondents' subjective interest in politics, frequency of political media consumption, perceived relevance of politics to daily life, and ideological self-placement, reflecting the cognitive and affective dimensions of political engagement.

construct_clusters.name: authoritarian_versus_democratic_values
construct_clusters.description: Captures respondents' underlying value orientations regarding rule of law, individual freedoms versus security, tolerance for extralegal action, and preference for strong leadership over institutional constraints, reflecting the authoritarian-democratic value dimension.

sources.citation: Booth, J. A., & Seligson, M. A. (2009). The Legitimacy Puzzle in Latin America: Political Support and Democracy in Eight Nations. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Directly theorizes and measures democratic legitimacy in Latin America, distinguishing between diffuse support for democracy as a system and specific satisfaction with democratic performance.
sources.key_finding: Democratic legitimacy in Latin America is multidimensional, with citizens simultaneously endorsing democracy in principle while expressing dissatisfaction with its performance, and these dimensions have distinct determinants.

sources.citation: Moreno, A. (2002). La sociedad mexicana y el cambio: Apoyo a la democracia y preferencia por la estabilidad. In A. Moreno (Ed.), El votante mexicano: Democracia, actitudes políticas y conducta electoral. Fondo de Cultura Económica.
sources.relevance: Analyzes Mexican citizens' normative commitment to democracy versus authoritarian alternatives in the context of Mexico's democratic transition.
sources.key_finding: Mexican support for democracy increased significantly after the 2000 alternation in power, but satisfaction with democratic functioning remained low, revealing a legitimacy gap.

sources.citation: Norris, P. (2011). Democratic Deficit: Critical Citizens Revisited. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical framework of 'critical citizens' who support democratic ideals but are dissatisfied with democratic practice, directly relevant to measuring both normative and evaluative dimensions.
sources.key_finding: Growing democratic deficits—gaps between democratic aspirations and perceived performance—are a global phenomenon that can either undermine or strengthen democracy depending on institutional context.

sources.citation: Schedler, A., & Sarsfield, R. (2007). Democrats with adjectives: Linking direct and indirect measures of democratic support. European Journal of Political Research, 46(5), 637–659.
sources.relevance: Examines the measurement of democratic support in Mexico using both direct (preference for democracy) and indirect (rejection of authoritarian alternatives) indicators.
sources.key_finding: Direct and indirect measures of democratic support tap into different attitudinal dimensions, and many Mexicans who express support for democracy also endorse authoritarian alternatives, complicating legitimacy measurement.

sources.citation: Latinobarómetro. (2021). Informe Latinobarómetro 2021. Corporación Latinobarómetro.
sources.relevance: Provides longitudinal comparative data on satisfaction with democracy and preference for democracy across Latin America, including Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Support for democracy in Latin America declined to historic lows by 2021, with Mexico showing particularly sharp drops in satisfaction with democratic performance.

sources.citation: Mishler, W., & Rose, R. (2001). What are the origins of political trust? Testing institutional and cultural theories in post-communist societies. Comparative Political Studies, 34(1), 30–62.
sources.relevance: Tests whether institutional trust derives from cultural predispositions or rational evaluations of institutional performance, directly relevant to the trust component of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Institutional trust is primarily driven by evaluations of institutional performance rather than deep cultural orientations, suggesting trust is responsive to governance quality.

sources.citation: Moreno, A. (2010). Confianza institucional en México. In A. Moreno (Ed.), La confianza en las instituciones: México en perspectiva comparada. Centro de Estudios Sociales y de Opinión Pública, Cámara de Diputados.
sources.relevance: Directly examines institutional trust in the Mexican context, analyzing trust in political parties, congress, judiciary, and other institutions.
sources.key_finding: Institutional trust in Mexico is generally low and varies significantly across institutions, with the military and church receiving higher trust than political parties and congress.

sources.citation: Niemi, R. G., Craig, S. C., & Mattei, F. (1991). Measuring internal political efficacy in the 1988 National Election Study. American Political Science Review, 85(4), 1407–1413.
sources.relevance: Provides foundational measurement framework for political efficacy, distinguishing internal (citizen competence) from external (government responsiveness) efficacy.
sources.key_finding: Internal and external political efficacy are empirically distinct dimensions that require separate measurement, with external efficacy more closely tied to institutional trust.

sources.citation: Cleary, M. R., & Stokes, S. C. (2006). Democracy and the Culture of Skepticism: Political Trust in Argentina and Mexico. Russell Sage Foundation.
sources.relevance: Directly compares institutional trust in Mexico and Argentina, examining how democratic experience shapes trust in government institutions.
sources.key_finding: In newer democracies like Mexico, political trust is more closely linked to partisanship and clientelistic networks than to evaluations of government performance.

sources.citation: Verba, S., Schlozman, K. L., & Brady, H. E. (1995). Voice and Equality: Civic Voluntarism in American Politics. Harvard University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides the foundational civic voluntarism model explaining participation through resources, engagement, and recruitment, applicable to measuring diverse forms of participation.
sources.key_finding: Political participation is driven by resources (time, money, civic skills), psychological engagement, and recruitment networks, with socioeconomic status as a key predictor.

sources.citation: Klesner, J. L. (2007). Social capital and political participation in Latin America: Evidence from Argentina, Chile, Mexico, and Peru. Latin American Research Review, 42(2), 1–32.
sources.relevance: Directly examines the relationship between social capital and political participation in Mexico and other Latin American countries.
sources.key_finding: Social capital—measured through associational membership and interpersonal trust—significantly predicts both conventional and unconventional political participation in Mexico.

sources.citation: Somuano, M. F. (2005). Más allá del voto: Modos de participación política no electoral en México. Foro Internacional, 45(1), 65–88.
sources.relevance: Directly analyzes non-electoral forms of political participation in Mexico, including community organizing, protest, and contacting officials.
sources.key_finding: Non-electoral participation in Mexico is shaped by education, political interest, and organizational membership, with significant variation across urban and rural contexts.

sources.citation: Holzner, C. A. (2010). Poverty of Democracy: The Institutional Roots of Political Participation in Mexico. University of Pittsburgh Press.
sources.relevance: Examines how institutional context and clientelism shape political participation patterns in Mexico, particularly among lower-income citizens.
sources.key_finding: Clientelistic mobilization substitutes for autonomous participation among Mexico's poor, creating a participation pattern that reinforces rather than challenges inequality.

sources.citation: Prior, M. (2010). You've either got it or you don't? The stability of political interest over the life cycle. Journal of Politics, 72(3), 747–766.
sources.relevance: Examines the stability and determinants of political interest as a core attitudinal dimension, relevant to measuring subjective interest and its relationship to media consumption.
sources.key_finding: Political interest is remarkably stable over the life course and is largely set by early adulthood, functioning as a dispositional trait rather than a situational response.

sources.citation: Moreno, A., & Méndez, P. (2007). Getting to know the Mexican voter. In J. I. Domínguez, C. Lawson, & A. Moreno (Eds.), Consolidating Mexico's Democracy: The 2006 Presidential Campaign in Comparative Perspective. Johns Hopkins University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes political interest, media consumption, and ideological self-placement among Mexican voters during a critical election.
sources.key_finding: Political interest in Mexico is strongly stratified by education and socioeconomic status, with media consumption patterns mediating the relationship between interest and political knowledge.

sources.citation: Zaller, J. R. (1992). The Nature and Origins of Mass Opinion. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical framework linking political awareness (interest + media consumption) to opinion formation, directly relevant to the cognitive engagement dimension.
sources.key_finding: Political awareness—a combination of interest and information exposure—is the key moderator of how citizens receive and accept political messages, shaping opinion formation.

sources.citation: Camp, R. A. (2014). Politics in Mexico: Democratic Consolidation or Decline? (6th ed.). Oxford University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides comprehensive analysis of Mexican political culture including political interest, ideological orientations, and media's role in political engagement.
sources.key_finding: Political interest in Mexico remains comparatively low by international standards, with significant urban-rural and generational divides in engagement patterns.

sources.citation: Inglehart, R., & Welzel, C. (2005). Modernization, Cultural Change, and Democracy: The Human Development Sequence. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides the foundational theory linking socioeconomic development to shifts from survival/authoritarian values toward self-expression/democratic values, directly relevant to the authoritarian-democratic value dimension.
sources.key_finding: Rising existential security drives a cultural shift from authoritarian to emancipative values, which in turn creates mass demand for democratic institutions and civil liberties.

sources.citation: Seligson, M. A., & Booth, J. A. (2009). Predicting coups? Democratic vulnerabilities, the AmericasBarometer and the 2009 Honduran crisis. Americas Barometer Insights, 2009(1).
sources.relevance: Uses survey measures of authoritarian attitudes and tolerance for extralegal action to assess democratic vulnerability in Latin America, directly relevant to measuring preferences for strong leadership over institutional constraints.
sources.key_finding: High levels of tolerance for executive coups and military intervention correlate with low institutional trust and perceived insecurity, identifying attitudinal preconditions for democratic breakdown.

sources.citation: Magaloni, B. (2006). Voting for Autocracy: Hegemonic Party Survival and its Demise in Mexico. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how authoritarian values and clientelistic attachments sustained one-party rule in Mexico, providing historical context for the persistence of authoritarian orientations.
sources.key_finding: Authoritarian values in Mexico were reinforced by the PRI's hegemonic party system through a combination of patronage, fear, and strategic coordination, leaving a lasting imprint on political culture.

sources.citation: Canache, D. (2012). Citizens' conceptualizations of democracy: Structural complexity, substantive content, and political significance. Comparative Political Studies, 45(9), 1132–1158.
sources.relevance: Examines how Latin American citizens conceptualize democracy, including whether they prioritize freedom, order, or economic outcomes, relevant to the values dimension.
sources.key_finding: Citizens who conceptualize democracy in terms of freedoms and rights are more likely to reject authoritarian alternatives, while those emphasizing order and economic outcomes show greater tolerance for authoritarianism.

sources.citation: Díaz Domínguez, A. (2020). Valores autoritarios y apoyo a la democracia en México. Política y Gobierno, 27(1), 1–28.
sources.relevance: Directly measures authoritarian value orientations in Mexico and their relationship to democratic support, using survey data comparable to the ENCUP framework.
sources.key_finding: Authoritarian values remain prevalent in Mexico, particularly among less educated and rural populations, and significantly predict tolerance for extralegal government actions and preference for strong-hand leadership.

variable_strategies.justification: Democratic legitimacy is inherently multi-dimensional, encompassing satisfaction with democracy, support for democratic institutions, and evaluations of democratic performance, so no single item captures it fully. The selected questions span evaluative and attitudinal dimensions of legitimacy and are selected for higher entropy (H≥2.12), ensuring sufficient variance for PCA to extract a meaningful first component. PCA is preferred over sum/mean scoring because the items have heterogeneous scale widths (K=5 to K=13), and the first principal component will weight items by their shared variance rather than treating all scales as equivalent.

Institutional trust and efficacy is a broad construct requiring multiple institutions and efficacy dimensions to be represented, so aggregation is necessary. The p84_x battery items share a common 6-point scale and tap trust in specific institutions, while p81_1 and p82_1 (K=13, highest entropy) capture broader systemic trust and efficacy perceptions; p63_1 and p46 add evaluative and efficacy facets. PCA is chosen because of the mixed scale widths (6-point battery vs. 7-point vs. 13-point items), allowing the first component to capture the dominant trust dimension without distortion from raw score differences.

Civic and political participation spans voting, organizational membership, protest, and community engagement, making it inherently multi-dimensional and requiring aggregation. The selected items cover distinct participation modes (electoral, associational, collective action) and are chosen for higher entropy (H≥1.89), ensuring they carry sufficient distributional information. PCA is preferred because scale widths vary (K=5 to K=14), and the first component will isolate the general participation propensity factor while downweighting low-variance or redundant items like p48_1 and p53_1 (H<1.0).

Political interest and engagement encompasses self-reported interest, media consumption, political discussion, and knowledge, requiring multiple items to avoid single-item bias. The selected questions have the highest entropy values (H≥1.89) and span cognitive engagement (interest, attention) and behavioral engagement (discussion, information-seeking), providing good construct coverage. PCA is appropriate given the heterogeneous scale widths (K=6 to K=14), and the first component will capture the latent general engagement factor that underlies these correlated but not identical indicators.

The authoritarian-democratic values dimension is a latent attitudinal construct that cannot be captured by any single item, as it encompasses preferences for strong leadership, rule of law, minority rights, and democratic norms. The selected items have the highest entropy (H≥1.74) and collectively span both the authoritarian pole (preference for strong leaders, order) and the democratic pole (support for rights, pluralism), ensuring the PCA first component reflects the full ideological continuum. PCA is preferred over sum scoring because scale widths are mixed (K=5 to K=7) and items at opposite poles of the construct need to be oriented consistently by the factor loadings rather than assumed to be additive.

construct_clusters.name: supernatural_belief_system
construct_clusters.description: Measures the breadth and content of an individual's supernatural and metaphysical beliefs, capturing both orthodox religious doctrines (God, heaven, hell, soul) and folk or syncretic beliefs (witchcraft, karma, reincarnation, limpias), which together index the structure of a person's cosmological worldview.

construct_clusters.name: religious_practice_and_devotion
construct_clusters.description: Captures the intensity of personal religious practice and devotional behavior, including prayer frequency, seeking comfort in religion, use of religious values in family life, reliance on religious figures for guidance, and ritual observance for life events.

construct_clusters.name: religious_socialization_and_identity
construct_clusters.description: Measures the degree to which religious identity was transmitted through family and formal education and remains stable across generations, reflecting the strength of religious socialization as a mechanism of cultural reproduction.

construct_clusters.name: church_state_separation_attitudes
construct_clusters.description: Assesses citizens' normative views on the appropriate boundaries between religious institutions and the public sphere, including opinions on churches in politics, use of public resources for religious events, church taxation, media religious content, and clergy involvement in political discourse.

construct_clusters.name: religious_tolerance_and_pluralism
construct_clusters.description: Measures an individual's acceptance of religious diversity and willingness to coexist with people of different faiths, including perceptions of freedom of religion, tolerance within family and community, and attitudes toward exclusion or expulsion based on religious minority status.

sources.citation: Stark, R. (2001). Gods, rituals, and the moral order. Journal for the Scientific Study of Religion, 40(4), 619–636.
sources.relevance: Provides a theoretical framework for understanding how belief in God, the afterlife, and supernatural agents structures moral cosmologies, directly relevant to measuring orthodox and heterodox supernatural beliefs.
sources.key_finding: Belief in a morally engaged God who rewards and punishes is more consequential for behavior than diffuse supernatural beliefs, suggesting the need to differentiate orthodox from folk beliefs.

sources.citation: De la Torre, R., & Cristina Gutiérrez Zúñiga, C. (2020). Encuesta Nacional sobre Creencias y Prácticas Religiosas en México (ENCREER/RIFREM 2016). In R. De la Torre & C. Gutiérrez Zúñiga (Eds.), Mismos credos, ¿mismas prácticas? CIESAS.
sources.relevance: Directly measures the breadth of supernatural beliefs among Mexicans—including God, saints, the devil, witchcraft, karma, and limpias—providing the empirical basis for a syncretic cosmological worldview construct in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Mexican believers hold layered cosmologies where orthodox Catholic beliefs coexist with folk and New Age beliefs such as karma, reincarnation, and spiritual cleansings (limpias).

sources.citation: Draper, S., & Baker, J. O. (2011). Themes in the study of paranormal beliefs: A content analysis. Journal for the Scientific Study of Religion, 50(4), 714–724.
sources.relevance: Reviews how paranormal and folk beliefs (witchcraft, astrology, reincarnation) relate to but are distinct from traditional religious beliefs, supporting the inclusion of both orthodox and heterodox items in a single construct.
sources.key_finding: Paranormal beliefs form a dimension partially independent of conventional religiosity, suggesting that a comprehensive supernatural belief measure must include both orthodox and non-orthodox items.

sources.citation: Chesnut, R. A. (2003). Competitive spirits: Latin America's new religious economy. Oxford University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the religious marketplace in Latin America, including Mexico, where Catholic, Pentecostal, and folk spiritual traditions compete and blend, contextualizing why syncretic belief measurement is essential.
sources.key_finding: Latin America's religious pluralism produces syncretic belief systems where individuals combine elements from multiple traditions, making single-tradition belief measures insufficient.

causal_relationships.related_construct: health_seeking_behavior (use of traditional medicine vs. biomedical care)

sources.citation: Stark, R., & Glock, C. Y. (1968). American piety: The nature of religious commitment. University of California Press.
sources.relevance: Foundational work defining the dimensions of religiosity including ritualistic (practice), experiential, and devotional dimensions, directly informing the measurement of prayer, worship attendance, and devotional behavior.
sources.key_finding: Religious commitment is multidimensional; the ritualistic and devotional dimensions (prayer frequency, worship attendance) are empirically distinguishable from belief and knowledge dimensions.

sources.citation: Pew Research Center. (2014). Religion in Latin America: Widespread change in a historically Catholic region. Pew Research Center.
sources.relevance: Provides large-scale survey data on religious practices in Mexico and Latin America, including prayer frequency, church attendance, and reliance on religious guidance, directly comparable to the items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Mexico shows high levels of personal devotion (daily prayer, seeking comfort in religion) even among those with declining institutional affiliation, highlighting the importance of measuring practice independently from identity.

sources.citation: Ellison, C. G., & Levin, J. S. (1998). The religion-health connection: Evidence, theory, and future directions. Health Education & Behavior, 25(6), 700–720.
sources.relevance: Reviews how religious practice (prayer, attendance) relates to health outcomes, establishing the construct's importance for cross-domain analysis with health and well-being.
sources.key_finding: Frequent religious practice, particularly prayer and attendance, is associated with better mental health and coping, mediated by social support and meaning-making mechanisms.

sources.citation: Suárez, H. J. (2015). Creyentes urbanos: Sociología de la experiencia religiosa en una colonia popular de la Ciudad de México. UNAM-IIS.
sources.relevance: Ethnographic and survey-based study of religious practice among urban poor Mexicans, showing how devotional behaviors (prayer, limpias, pilgrimages) structure daily life and family socialization.
sources.key_finding: Among urban popular sectors in Mexico, religious devotion is deeply embedded in family routines and crisis coping, functioning as a primary resource for navigating poverty and insecurity.

sources.citation: Sherkat, D. E. (2003). Religious socialization: Sources of influence and influences of agency. In M. Dillon (Ed.), Handbook of the sociology of religion (pp. 151–163). Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive theoretical framework for understanding how family, education, and community transmit religious identity across generations, directly relevant to items on parental religion, religious education, and identity stability.
sources.key_finding: Religious socialization is primarily driven by parental modeling and religious education, with family religious homogamy being the strongest predictor of intergenerational religious transmission.

sources.citation: Voas, D., & Crockett, A. (2005). Religion in Britain: Neither believing nor belonging. Sociology, 39(1), 11–28.
sources.relevance: Examines intergenerational decline in religious identity and practice, providing a secularization framework applicable to measuring whether Mexican religious identity remains stable or erodes across generations.
sources.key_finding: Each successive generation shows roughly half the religiosity of its parents, suggesting that religious socialization weakens over time in modernizing societies.

sources.citation: De la Torre, R., & Gutiérrez Zúñiga, C. (2007). Atlas de la diversidad religiosa en México. CIESAS/El Colegio de Jalisco.
sources.relevance: Maps religious diversity and identity transmission across Mexican regions, documenting how Catholic socialization varies by geography, ethnicity, and urbanization, directly contextualizing the construct for Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Religious identity in Mexico is strongly shaped by regional Catholic traditions and family socialization, but shows increasing diversification in urban and border areas.

sources.citation: Hervieu-Léger, D. (2000). Religion as a chain of memory. Rutgers University Press.
